title: Sequences and indexes

publication.changeSummary: Topic-specific draft for Sequences and indexes; requires human source, technical, pedagogy, and accessibility review. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: CS2023 Software Development Fundamentals; August 2023; section applicable to Sequences and indexes
sources.claim: A sequence stores ordered elements at valid positions; indexed access, update, slicing, and traversal must respect empty input and the first and last valid indexes.

canonicalExplanation.summary: A sequence stores ordered elements at valid positions; indexed access, update, slicing, and traversal must respect empty input and the first and last valid indexes.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: For four items with zero-based indexing, valid indexes are zero through three and a loop condition index less than length visits each exactly once. Your goal is to trace indexed reads, updates and traversal without off-by-one errors. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Iteration; Values and types. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Operational model
sections.body: A sequence stores ordered elements at valid positions; indexed access, update, slicing, and traversal must respect empty input and the first and last valid indexes. The learner should use this model to predict an observable result rather than merely repeat terminology.

sections.heading: Boundary and failure point
sections.body: Index origins, negative indexes, slicing bounds, mutability, and out-of-range behavior differ by language and sequence type. A correct explanation therefore includes the applicable assumptions and avoids this common mistake: A sequence of length four has a valid element at index four because length names the last position.

includes: Use ordered collections and valid positions, including empty and boundary cases.
Trace indexed reads, updates and traversal without off-by-one errors.
excludes: Index origins, negative indexes, slicing bounds, mutability, and out-of-range behavior differ by language and sequence type.

outcomes: Trace indexed reads, updates and traversal without off-by-one errors.

examples.title: Sequences and indexes: direct case
examples.situation: For four items with zero-based indexing, valid indexes are zero through three and a loop condition index less than length visits each exactly once.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Observe the starting evidence: For four items with zero-based indexing, valid indexes are zero through three and a loop condition index less than length visits each exactly once.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Check the result against this correction: For a zero-based sequence, the last valid index is length minus one; test empty, first, last, and one-past-last cases explicitly.
examples.result: The case supports the declared outcome because For a zero-based sequence, the last valid index is length minus one; test empty, first, last, and one-past-last cases explicitly.

examples.title: Sequences and indexes: boundary case
examples.situation: Removing the first queue item changes later positions, so cached indexes must be reconsidered before another access.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Identify the changed context: Removing the first queue item changes later positions, so cached indexes must be reconsidered before another access.
Step 2 — Keep this boundary explicit: Index origins, negative indexes, slicing bounds, mutability, and out-of-range behavior differ by language and sequence type.
Step 3 — Reject the documented misconception: A sequence of length four has a valid element at index four because length names the last position.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Trace indexed reads, updates and traversal without off-by-one errors.

misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Using the direct and boundary scenarios, explain why this belief fails: A sequence of length four has a valid element at index four because length names the last position.

analogy.example: An organized storeroom can model Sequences and indexes: shelves preserve order, labeled bins group fields, membership lists avoid duplicates, and indexes locate values.
usefulFor: Comparing organization by order, name, membership, lookup, and update needs
limitations: Physical storage hides concrete complexity, equality, hashing, mutation, allocation, and concurrency guarantees.
Index origins, negative indexes, slicing bounds, mutability, and out-of-range behavior differ by language and sequence type.
